meta.name: Docs
meta.description: A clean documentation template for EmDash CMS. Perfect for product docs, API references, and developer guides.
meta.author: EmDash Templates

children.text: Acme SDK is a powerful toolkit for building modern applications. This documentation is built with EmDash CMS.

data.title: Introduction
data.description: What Acme SDK is and why you should use it

children.text: TypeScript SDK for the Acme API platform. It provides a clean, intuitive interface for interacting with all Acme services — from resource management to real-time event streaming.
— Every method, parameter, and response is fully typed. Your editor will autocomplete every field and catch errors before runtime.
— The SDK ships under 12 KB gzipped with zero runtime dependencies. It won't bloat your bundle.
— Works in Node.js 18+, Deno, Bun, and all modern browsers. One import, any environment.
— Automatic retries with exponential backoff, plus structured error types you can pattern-match against.
Here's what working with Acme SDK looks like:
This documentation covers everything from installation and initial setup to advanced patterns like pagination, streaming, and error recovery. Start with the
guide to get the SDK added to your project, then follow the
to make your first API call in under five minutes.

data.title: Installation
data.description: Install and set up the Acme SDK in your project

children.text: Install Acme SDK using your preferred package manager:
and other modern APIs that require Node.js 18+.
— Required for full type inference support. The SDK uses
type parameters and other features from TS 5.0.
Acme SDK has no required peer dependencies. However, if you plan to use the
module, you will need to install
Create a test file to confirm everything is wired up correctly:

import { AcmeClient, version } from '@acme/sdk';

console.log(`Acme SDK v${version} loaded successfully`);
. If you see the version printed, you're ready to continue to the Quick Start guide.

data.title: Quick Start
data.description: Make your first API call in under five minutes

children.text: In this guide you will install the SDK, create a client instance, and make your first API request. By the end you'll have a working script that lists your Acme projects.
If you haven't already, add the SDK to your project:

Now that you have a working setup, explore the

data.title: Configuration
data.description: Customize client behavior with options and environment variables

children.text: constructor accepts a configuration object with the following options:
(string, required) — Your Acme API key. Starts with
(string, optional) — Override the default API base URL. Defaults to
. Useful for proxied or self-hosted environments.
(number, optional) — Request timeout in milliseconds. Defaults to
(number, optional) — Maximum number of automatic retries on transient errors (5xx, network failures). Defaults to
, logs all HTTP requests and responses to the console. Defaults to
environment variable. If set, you can create a client without passing

// Reads ACME_API_KEY from environment automatically
const client = new AcmeClient();
as environment variables. Explicit constructor options always take precedence over environment variables.
For projects that need shared configuration, create an
Then import it wherever you create a client:
You can override configuration per-request using the
parameter available on every method. This is useful for one-off timeout adjustments or adding custom headers:

data.title: Authentication
data.description: Learn how to authenticate with the Acme SDK using API keys, OAuth 2.0, and token refresh strategies.

children.text: The Acme SDK supports multiple authentication methods depending on your use case. This guide covers API key authentication for server-side applications and OAuth 2.0 for user-facing integrations.
The simplest way to authenticate is with an API key. Generate one from your Acme Dashboard under Settings > API Keys. Pass it when initializing the client:
For applications that act on behalf of users, use the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow. First, redirect the user to the authorization URL:
After the user authorizes, exchange the code for an access token:
Access tokens expire after 1 hour. Use the
to obtain a new access token without requiring the user to re-authorize. The SDK handles this automatically when you enable
Never expose API keys in client-side code or commit them to version control. Use environment variables and a
file for local development. Rotate keys periodically and use scoped tokens with the minimum required permissions.

data.title: Data Fetching
data.description: Query, filter, and paginate data efficiently with the Acme SDK.

children.text: The Acme SDK provides a fluent API for querying resources. This guide covers basic queries, filtering, pagination, and caching strategies for optimal performance.
Fetch a single resource by ID or retrieve a collection. All query methods return typed responses:

const resource = await client.resources.get('res_abc123');
const allResources = await client.resources.list();
option to filter results. Filters support equality, comparison operators, and logical combinators:
For large datasets, use cursor-based pagination. Each response includes a
field when more results are available:
The SDK supports built-in response caching. Enable it by passing a
option to the client. The default TTL is 60 seconds. For server-side applications, consider using a shared cache like Redis by implementing the

data.title: Error Handling
data.description: Handle errors gracefully with typed error classes and retry strategies.

children.text: The Acme SDK throws typed errors that make it easy to handle specific failure cases. All errors extend the base AcmeError class and include an error code, message, and optional metadata.
— Base error class. All SDK errors inherit from this. Contains
— Thrown when request parameters fail validation. The
array contains field-level details about what went wrong.
— Thrown when you exceed the API rate limit. Includes
(in seconds) indicating when you can retry the request.
Use instanceof checks to handle specific error types:
(401) — The API key is missing or invalid.
(404) — The requested resource does not exist.
Enable automatic retries for transient errors by setting
in the client configuration. The SDK uses exponential backoff and automatically retries on 429 and 5xx status codes:

data.title: Deployment
data.description: Deploy your Acme SDK integration to Cloudflare Workers, Vercel, or a Node.js server.

children.text: This guide covers deploying applications that use the Acme SDK across different environments and platforms.
to control which API endpoint the SDK targets.
The SDK is fully compatible with the Workers runtime. Store your API key as a secret using
and access it from the environment bindings:
Add your API key in the Vercel dashboard under Settings > Environment Variables. The SDK works in both Serverless Functions and Edge Functions. For Edge, make sure to import from
For traditional Node.js deployments, load environment variables from a
file in development and from your hosting provider's secret management in production:
— Target environment: development, staging, or production. Defaults to production.
— Override the API base URL for self-hosted instances.
— Request timeout in milliseconds. Defaults to 30000.

data.title: Client
data.description: API reference for the AcmeClient class, constructor options, and core methods.

children.text: class is the main entry point for interacting with the Acme API. It manages authentication, connection pooling, and provides access to all resource endpoints.
(number) — Request timeout in ms. Defaults to 30000.
(number) — Maximum retry attempts for failed requests. Defaults to 0.
(CacheConfig | false) — Cache configuration or false to disable.
— Establishes a persistent connection to the Acme API. Required when using WebSocket-based real-time subscriptions. For standard REST calls, connection is handled automatically.
— Closes the persistent connection and cleans up resources. Always call this when shutting down your application to avoid connection leaks.
— Returns the current connection status. Possible values:

data.title: Endpoints
data.description: Complete reference for all available API endpoints including Resources and Users.

children.text: All endpoints are accessed through the AcmeClient instance. Each endpoint group provides standard CRUD methods with full TypeScript support.
— Retrieves a paginated list of resources. Options include
— Retrieves a single resource by its unique ID. Throws
if the resource does not exist.
— Creates a new resource. Required fields:
— Updates an existing resource. Supports partial updates — only provided fields are modified.
— Permanently deletes a resource. This action cannot be undone.
— Retrieves a paginated list of users. Supports the same filtering and ordering options as resources.
— Retrieves a user by ID. Returns the full user profile including
— Creates a new user. Required fields:

data.title: Types
data.description: TypeScript type definitions for all SDK interfaces, response types, and error classes.

children.text: The Acme SDK is written in TypeScript and exports all types for use in your application. This reference covers the core types you will work with.
configuration option with pluggable adapters. Added cursor-based pagination helpers. Improved TypeScript generics for better type inference on filtered queries.
Major release with breaking changes. Migrated to native
— dropped the axios dependency. New error class hierarchy with
base class. Minimum Node.js version raised to 18. OAuth 2.0 support added.
Added Users endpoints. Improved retry logic with exponential backoff. Added
Initial release with Resources CRUD, API key authentication, automatic retries, and full TypeScript support.
